S05T.title: Demographic parity
S05T.lhs: How often the model says “yes” to group A.
S05T.rhs: How often it says “yes” to group B.
S05T.claim: The equals sign demands these two rates be the same — not close, equal.

example.scenario: A bank approves loans at 40% in neighborhood A, 22% in B.
example.cost: Group B is handed loans far less often, regardless of who would repay.

S05T.values_claim: Outcomes should be equal regardless of base rates — redress over conditional accuracy.
S05T.reentry: That's demographic parity. Back to the three definitions.
S05T.narration: Before we move on, let's actually read it. The left side is how often the model says yes to group A; the right side, how often it says yes to group B; and the equals sign demands those two rates be the same — not close, equal. Y-hat is the model's prediction, a one or a zero; A is the group; little a and little b are the two groups; P just means the probability of. So picture a bank that approves loans at forty percent in neighborhood A but twenty-two percent in B — forty does not equal twenty-two, parity is violated, and group B is handed loans far less often regardless of who would actually repay. The value buried in here: outcomes should be equal regardless of base rates. That's demographic parity. Now, back to the three definitions.

S07T.title: Equalized odds
S07T.latex: P(\hat{Y}=1 \mid Y=1, A=a) = P(\hat{Y}=1 \mid Y=1, A=b)
S07T.lhs: Among people who truly are positive, how often group A is caught.
S07T.rhs: The same catch-rate for group B.
S07T.claim: Equal — and the very same demand repeats for the false-alarm rate.

example.scenario: Of qualified applicants, a screen advances 90% in A, 70% in B.
example.cost: Qualified people in group B are passed over more often.

S07T.values_claim: Fairness means the model's errors fall equally on both groups, even when base rates differ.
S07T.reentry: That's equalized odds. Back to the metrics.
S07T.narration: Let's read it. The left side is, among the people who truly are positive, how often the model catches group A; the right side is the same catch-rate for group B; and equalized odds demands they match — plus the identical demand on the false-alarm rate among the truly negative. Watch the two Y's: capital Y is the real label, Y-hat is the model's guess — keep them apart. So if a hiring screen advances ninety percent of the qualified people in group A but only seventy percent in B, ninety doesn't equal seventy — violated — and qualified people in B get passed over. The value here: the errors must fall equally. That's equalized odds. Back to the metrics.

S09T.title: Calibration
S09T.latex: P(Y=1 \mid \hat{P}=p,\, A=a) = P(Y=1 \mid \hat{P}=p,\, A=b) = p \quad \forall p
S09T.lhs: Among everyone scored p, how many in A truly are positive.
S09T.rhs: The same for B — and both must equal the score p itself.
S09T.claim: “= p for all p”: the score must mean what it says, identically per group.

example.scenario: Among people scored 0.7, 70% of A repay — but only 55% of B.
example.cost: An 0.7 means different things by group; the score itself is biased.

S09T.values_claim: Fairness means the score is equally honest per group — truthful meaning over equal selection.
S09T.reentry: That's calibration. Back to the metrics.
S09T.narration: Read it in words first. The left side is, among everyone the model scored p, how many in group A actually turn out positive; the right side, the same for B; and both must equal p itself, for every p. P-hat is the score the model emits; little p is a particular score level; the upside-down A means for all. So among people scored zero-point-seven, suppose seventy percent of group A repay but only fifty-five percent of B — then zero-point-seven means two different things, and the score is biased. The value here: the number must be equally honest for everyone. That's calibration. Back to the metrics.

S14T.title: Precision (PPV)
S14T.lhs: Precision: of those flagged positive, the share who truly are.
S14T.rhs: Built from the base rate and the two error rates.
S14T.claim: This is a definition — and notice the base rate p sits right on top.

example.scenario: Same TPR and FPR; base rate 0.6 in A versus 0.3 in B.
example.cost: The identical model looks far less precise where positives are rarer.

S14T.reentry: That's why precision tracks the base rate. Back to the arithmetic.
S14T.narration: This one's a definition, so read what it builds. The left side is precision — of everyone the model flags positive, how many really are. The right side builds that from the base rate and the two error rates, and the thing to notice is the base rate p sitting right on top. So hold the model fixed — same true-positive and false-positive rates — and just change the base rate from zero-point-six to zero-point-three: precision falls from about zero-point-seven to about zero-point-four. Same model, far less precise where positives are rarer. That's the lever behind the whole impossibility. Back to the arithmetic.

S16T.title: Chouldechova's identity
S16T.lhs: The odds that a flagged person truly is positive.
S16T.rhs: The base-rate odds times the model's hit-to-false ratio.
S16T.claim: An identity — it always holds; so equal precision forces an error-rate trade.

example.scenario: Two groups, base rates 0.6 and 0.3, with equal precision demanded.
example.compare: forces t/f to differ by group
example.cost: You cannot equalize precision and error rates at the same time.

S16T.reentry: That's the algebra behind the impossibility. Back to it.
S16T.narration: This is an identity — it falls out of Bayes and always holds, so read what it forces. The left side is the odds a flagged person truly is positive — that's just precision rewritten. The right side is the base-rate odds times the model's hit-to-false ratio. Because the equals sign always holds, if you demand equal precision across two groups with different base rates, the hit-to-false ratio is forced to differ between them. In plain terms: you cannot equalize precision and equalize the error rates at once. That's the algebra under the impossibility theorem. Back to it.

S24T.title: Lipschitz condition
S24T.latex: D\big(M(x),\, M(y)\big) \le d(x, y) \quad \forall\, x, y
S24T.lhs: How differently the model treats two people.
S24T.rhs: How different those two people actually are.
S24T.claim: The “≤” is the whole point: the treatment gap can be no bigger than the person gap.

example.scenario: Two applicants, identical finances, different ZIP. If d ignores ZIP, d = 0.
example.cost: But if d counts ZIP, unequal treatment becomes “allowed.”

S24T.values_claim: Fairness means similar people get similar treatment — and someone must define “similar.”
S24T.reentry: That's individual fairness. Back to it.
S24T.narration: This one is an inequality, and the inequality is the whole idea. The left side is how differently the model treats two people; the right side is how different those two people actually are; and the less-than-or-equal sign says the treatment gap can be no bigger than the person gap. M is the model, big D is distance between its outputs, little d is distance between people. So take two applicants with identical finances and different ZIP codes — if your distance d ignores ZIP, then d is zero, and the model is forced to treat them identically. But if d counts ZIP as a real difference, unequal treatment quietly becomes allowed. The value: similar people should be treated similarly — and someone has to define similar. Back to it.

S29T.title: Counterfactual fairness
S29T.latex: P\!\left(\hat{Y}_{A\leftarrow a}(U)=y \mid X=x, A=a\right) = P\!\left(\hat{Y}_{A\leftarrow a'}(U)=y \mid X=x, A=a\right)
S29T.lhs: This person's decision in their actual group.
S29T.rhs: Their decision if we'd flipped their group, all else held.
S29T.claim: Equal — flipping the group must not change the decision.

glossary.mean: prediction in a world where A is set to a
their group, and the flipped group

example.scenario: A loan model “uses only education,” but history tied race to credential.
example.compare: flip race ⇒ credential changes ⇒ decision changes
example.cost: So a “race-blind” model still treats the person differently.

S29T.values_claim: Fairness means a counterfactual change of group leaves the decision untouched — a strong causal assumption about the world.
S29T.reentry: That's counterfactual fairness. Back to it.
S29T.narration: This reads like a thought experiment. The left side is this exact person's decision in their actual group; the right side is their decision in a world where we flip their group and hold everything else fixed; and the equals sign demands those be the same. The arrow A-left-arrow-a means we intervene and set the group; U is everything personal we hold constant. So take a loan model that only looks at education — but historically, access to that education was shaped by race. Flip the person's race, and their credential changes too, so the decision changes. A race-blind model still treats them differently. The value, and it's a strong one: a counterfactual change of group should leave the decision untouched. Back to it.

S32T.title: Generalized Entropy Index
S32T.lhs: One number for the total unfairness.
S32T.rhs: How spread-out each person's “benefit” is around the average.
S32T.claim: A definition — and the knob α decides whose gap counts most.

example.scenario: Same spread of benefit, read at α = 0 versus α = 2.
example.compare: α=0 weights the bottom; α=2 the top
example.cost: Choosing α chooses whose harm the number is most sensitive to.

S32T.values_claim: Picking α is itself a value choice about whose harm matters most.
S32T.reentry: That's the entropy index. Back to it.
S32T.narration: Don't let the formula scare you — read what it does. The left side is a single number for total unfairness. The right side measures how spread out each person's benefit is around the average — straight from income-inequality economics. Little b-i is each person's benefit, mu is the average, and the one to watch is alpha, the exponent. Alpha is a knob: take the same spread of benefit and read it at alpha equals zero versus alpha equals two — a low alpha makes the index scream about the worst-served at the bottom, a high alpha responds more to the top. So picking alpha is itself a value choice about whose harm the number cares about most. That's the entropy index. Back to it.
